questions.prompt: Why is exec form CMD preferred over shell form in production?

choices.text: Signal handling. Shell form wraps in /bin/sh -c; your app runs as a child of sh, not as PID 1. SIGTERM goes to sh, which usually doesn't forward it. Your app gets no graceful-shutdown signal, drops in-flight requests, eventually SIGKILL'd. Exec form runs the binary directly as PID 1; signals reach it; clean shutdown. Universal rule: exec form in production.

questions.explanation: Exec form = direct exec, PID 1, signals work. Shell form breaks signal forwarding.

questions.prompt: Why does `COPY . .` before `RUN npm install` ruin build speed?

choices.text: Cache cascade. Docker's cache invalidates on COPY when any file in the context changes. With COPY before install, every source change reinvalidates the cache, forcing npm install to rerun. Fix: copy package.json/lock first, run install (cached when lock unchanged), then copy source. Builds drop from minutes to seconds for source-only changes.

questions.explanation: Lock-files-then-install-then-source ordering preserves cache. Universal Dockerfile discipline.

questions.prompt: What does multi-stage build actually achieve?

choices.text: Build tools in one stage, runtime artifacts in the final stage. The builder stage has compilers, dev deps, source code; the final stage starts from a minimal base and only COPYs the artifacts the running app needs. Build stage layers are discarded. Result: small production images (10-100x smaller for many workloads) with reproducible builds (still inside Docker). Universal pattern for modern Dockerfiles.

questions.explanation: Multi-stage separates build from runtime. Small, secure final images with reproducible builds.

questions.prompt: A Python app on Alpine has 20-minute builds because pip compiles NumPy from source. Fix?

choices.text: Switch to python:3.12-slim (Debian-based). Alpine uses musl libc; many Python packages with native code (NumPy, Pandas, lxml, cryptography) don't have musl wheels and must compile from source — slow. Debian-slim uses glibc; pip pulls prebuilt manylinux wheels — installs in seconds. Slightly larger image, dramatically faster builds. Right base for native-dependency workloads.

questions.explanation: Alpine = musl; native-dep Python apps want glibc (Debian-slim) for prebuilt wheels.

questions.prompt: Why does an SSH key COPY'd then RM'd in a Dockerfile still leak?

choices.text: Layers are immutable. Deleting in a later layer creates a whiteout but leaves the file in the earlier layer where it was added. Anyone with the image can extract it via `docker save` or `dive`. Fix: BuildKit `--secret` flag (secret mounted only during the RUN, never persisted in any layer) or multi-stage builds where credentials only live in discarded stages. Rotate any keys that have ever been in a published image.
rm is a no-op in Docker.

questions.explanation: Layer immutability: secrets in any layer persist regardless of later deletion. Use BuildKit secrets.
